Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 18 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Something changed! Looking at the image, the blue square on the left side now has a red part below it. Let me check what changed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed
before = last_transition.before_frame.ascii.split('\n')
after = last_transition.after_frame.ascii.split('\n')

print("=== CHANGED ROWS ===")
for i in range(64):
    if before[i] != after[i]:
        print(f"Row {i}:")
        # Show only the changed region
        for j in range(64):
            if before[i][j] != after[i][j]:
                start = max(0, j-2)
                end = min(64, j+3)
                print(f"  BEFORE[{start}:{end}]: {before[i][start:end]}")
                print(f"  AFTER[{start}:{end}]:  {after[i][start:end]}")
                break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! Clicking on the red cross target caused:
1. Rows 24-29, cols 18-20: Changed from charcoal (c) to red (R) - this is below the blue square
2. Rows 30-33, cols 10-14: Changed from red (R) to charcoal (c) - this is the red part of the horizontal bar
3. Row 63: Timer changed
So clicking on the red target moved the red object from the horizontal bar (rows 30-33, cols 12-17) to below the blue square (rows 24-29, cols 18-20). The red object is now a 6x3 vertical bar below the blue square.
This suggests that clicking on the red target "moves" the red object to a different position. Similarly, clicking on the blue target might move the blue object.
Let me check the current state of the left side objects. The blue square is at rows 20-23, cols 18-21. The red object is now at rows 24-29, cols 18-20 (below the blue square).
Wait, looking at the image, the blue square now has a red part below it. The blue square is at rows 20-23, cols 18-21, and the red object is at rows 24-29, cols 18-20. So the red object is now below the blue square, forming a vertical stack.
This is interesting. The game seems to be about rearranging objects on the left side by clicking on the targets on the right side. The red and blue targets toggle the position of the red and blue objects.
Let me check the initial state of the horizontal bar. In the initial state, the horizontal bar was:
- Gray 4x4 (rows 30-33, cols 8-11)
- Red 4x6 (rows 30-33, cols 12-17)
- Dark red 4x4 (rows 30-33, cols 18-21)
After clicking on the red target, the red object moved from the horizontal bar to below the blue square. The horizontal bar now has:
- Gray 4x4 (rows 30-33, cols 8-11)
- Charcoal 4x6 (rows 30-33, cols 12-17) - the red object was here
- Dark red 4x4 (rows 30-33, cols 18-21)
So the red object moved from the horizontal bar to below the blue square. This suggests that clicking on the red target "moves" the red object to a different position.
Let me try clicking on the red target again to see if it moves the red object back to the horizontal bar.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me check the current state of the left side
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("=== LEFT SIDE OBJECTS ===")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['pixels'] > 4 and node['pixels'] < 100:
        # Check if it's on the left side (col < 32)
        boundary = node['boundary']
        min_col = min(p[1] for p in boundary)
        if min_col < 32:
            print(f"  Color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, boundary={boundary}")

# Let me try clicking on the red target again to see if it toggles
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 19, 'col': 48}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
